hypothesis: no-op self-test
cache casted RMSNorm weight to avoid per-call dtype copies
enable built-in torch.compile
enable torch.compile (re-judged: argmax+JS gate)
enable torch.compile (Pareto gate, argmax+JS)
torch.compile for large inputs only (rows>=8192)
torch.compile large-only; regression=confidently-slower gate

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward runs per scaled-attention call (ICL layers, dist embedder, decoder). It currently issues 3 full-size [B,S,H,D] elementwise kernels: (1+tanh) add, base_scales*modulation mul, q*scales mul. Reassociate the algebraically-equal expression q*base_scales*(1+tanh) into a single mul (q*base_scales) followed by a fused torch.addcmul(a, a, tanh), removing one elementwise kernel launch per call. On this launch-bound model (GPU busy 6-57%), fewer kernel launches reduce overhead. Pure fp reordering (distribution of the multiply), which the referee's gate tolerates; argmax/JS stay within run-to-run noise.

Remove redundant per-call .contiguous() copies of q/k/v in the shared SDPA helper that every TabPFNV3 attention routes through. These tensors are permute(0,2,1,3) views of (B,S,H,D)-contiguous projection outputs, so the head (last) dim already has stride 1 — the only contiguity PyTorch's flash/efficient SDPA backends require. The .contiguous() therefore forced a full memory copy (the #1 GPU op: aten::copy_, 7.974ms, and ~3 large copies per flash call) without changing backend selection or math. Removing it eliminates ~498 large copies per predict in the launch/copy-bound regime. Output is bit-identical: flash is still selected (last-dim stride 1 satisfied) and the kernel math is independent of batch/head/seq strides. Batch slicing for the >65k-grid workaround keeps last-dim stride 1. Expected mechanism: fewer copy kernels + bytes moved → faster, especially on copy-dominated small inputs.

In FeatureDistributionEmbedder, each InducedSelfAttentionBlock copies its input (transpose(1,2).contiguous(), line 1264) AND its output (transpose(1,2).contiguous(), line 1286). Between consecutive layers these are inverse operations: the output is (B,R,C,E)-contiguous, and the next block transposes it back to (B,C,R,E) and re-copies. Returning a view instead (drop the output .contiguous()) means the next block's transpose(1,2) recovers the already-contiguous (B,C,R,E) storage, so its .contiguous() returns self (no kernel/copy). out_BCRE is contiguous in all paths (Linear/out-proj output reshaped, return_hidden tensor, or the in-place chunked buffer), so the recovered transpose is genuinely contiguous. Net: eliminates ~2 large aten::copy_ per layer boundary (copy is the #1 GPU op, launch/copy-bound regime). Bit-exact: only the physical layout of the handoff tensor changes; cat/Linear downstream accept strided inputs. Expected: fewer copy kernels + bytes moved on every dist-embed layer, helping both deterministic (typical) and small (sample) points without any fp reordering.

_DtypeMatchingRMSNorm.forward calls self.weight.to(input.dtype) on every invocation (~398/predict), each a kernel launch + small copy. In inference the weight is frozen, so the casted result is identical every call. Cache the casted weight keyed on the source weight identity, its ._version, and the target dtype; reuse it when unchanged. This removes ~398 redundant cast/copy kernel launches per predict in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime while producing a bit-identical casted tensor (same .to() math, same F.rms_norm call). The cache path is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() so the compiled large-input (sample) graph is unchanged (no python-side-effect graph break). Expected: fewer launches → faster typical, neutral sample.

apply_rope's final combine `t*cos + t_rotated*sin` issues two separate elementwise `mul` kernels plus an `add` kernel on the full (...,S,D) tensor, and it runs twice (q and k) per ColumnAggregator TransformerBlock across all blocks. Replacing it with `torch.addcmul(t*cos, t_rotated, sin)` fuses the second multiply and the add into a single kernel, eliminating one full-size elementwise launch per RoPE call. On this launch/elementwise-bound small model (aten::mul is 6.8ms/1181 calls, aten::add 3.1ms; GPU only 6-57% busy), fewer kernel launches reduce overhead. This is pure fp reassociation (distribution/ordering of a multiply-add), which the referee's argmax+JS gate tolerates; outputs stay within run-to-run noise. Applies to both interleaved and non-interleaved branches via the shared return.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward issues three pointwise kernels per scaled-attention call: `1 + tanh(...)` (add), `base_scales * modulation` (mul), and `q * scales` (mul). It runs on every CrossAttentionBlock-with-scaling (dist embedder), every ICL block, and the decoder. Replacing `base_scales * (1 + tanh)` with `torch.addcmul(base_scales, base_scales, tanh)` fuses the add and one multiply into a single kernel (base_scales + base_scales*tanh == base_scales*(1+tanh)), eliminating one full-size elementwise launch per call in this launch/elementwise-bound regime (aten::mul 6.8ms/1181, aten::add 3.1ms; GPU 6-57% busy). Unlike iteration 4's RoPE addcmul (which mixed bf16 t_rotated with fp32 sin and hit a slow fallback), here base_scales, the query-MLP output, and tanh all share q_BSHD's dtype, so addcmul stays on its fused same-dtype path. This is pure fp redistribution of a multiply-add (distribution, not reassociation — mathematically identical), which the argmax+JS gate tolerates; outputs stay within noise. Expected mechanism: fewer kernel launches → faster typical (eager, launch-bound) and neutral sample.

apply_rope recomputes cos/sin (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 small kernels) on every call. A single RotaryEmbedding instance is shared across all ColumnAggregator blocks (self.rope), and within each Attention layer it's invoked for both q and k, so the SAME cos/sin (depending only on seq_len, device, frozen inv_freq, interleaved) are regenerated 2×num_layers times per forward. Memoize them in a per-function dict keyed on (id(inv_freq), inv_freq._version, seq_len, device, interleaved); reuse when unchanged. This collapses all those redundant cos/sin builds to one per distinct (seq_len) per predict, cutting many small kernel launches in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. Bit-exact: cached cos/sin are computed by the identical ops and used read-only in the identical `t*cos + t_rotated*sin` arithmetic (only t_rotated, which depends on t, stays per-call). The cache is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() (the prior reason it wasn't cached), so the compiled large/sample graph is unchanged with no python-side-effect graph break. Expected: fewer launches → faster typical, neutral sample.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward recomputes `base_scales = base_mlp(_safe_log_seqlen(n))` on every scaled-attention call, but base_scales depends ONLY on (n, dtype, device) and the frozen inference weights -- never on the query. That tiny chain (~8 small kernels: ones/mul/clamp/log/cast + two Linears + GELU + view) runs per ICL block, per dist-embedder cross-attn block (per col/row chunk), per decoder call, and the same modules are invoked with the same n across the harness's repeated timed predicts. Caching base_scales per (n,dtype,device) on the module removes all those redundant tiny launches from steady-state timed runs in this launch-bound regime (GPU 6-57% busy). Output-preserving and bit-exact: the cached tensor is exactly what base_mlp would recompute (deterministic, frozen weights); the per-query path (query_mlp/tanh/mul) is unchanged. Caching is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() (compiled graph identical) and during training (weights mutate). Distinct from the prior rejected SoftmaxScaling edits, which only reassociated the elementwise muls; this eliminates kernel launches instead.

_safe_log_seqlen runs on every scaled-attention call (each ICL block, each dist-embedder cross-attn block, and the decoder). Its eager path materialises log(n) through ~5 tiny GPU kernels (torch.ones -> mul -> clamp -> log -> to). When `n` is a concrete Python int (eager, non-compiled = the `typical` operating point), I compute log(max(n,1)) on the host and create the 0-d result in one torch.full call, eliminating ~4 tiny kernel launches per scaled-attention call in this launch-bound regime (GPU 6-57% busy). The symbolic SymInt path is preserved exactly under torch.compiler.is_compiling(), so the compiled `sample` graph is byte-identical (no new guards, no recompiles) and stays neutral. Output-preserving: same clamped log(n) cast to the same dtype; only the host-vs-device evaluation of a scalar log differs (fp reordering the gate tolerates), so argmax/JS stay within noise.

In ManyClassDecoder.forward, one_hot_targets_BNHT = unsqueeze(2).expand(...,H,...).contiguous() materializes a full copy of the large (B, N, H, T) one-hot value tensor. It is then passed straight into _chunked_class_attention, which does F.pad / reshape / permute / reshape().contiguous() — each of which already produces a fresh contiguous tensor from the broadcast view. The explicit .contiguous() is therefore a redundant second materialization of a large tensor (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op). Dropping it lets the broadcast view flow into the existing pad/reshape, which copies it exactly once. Output is bit-identical (reshape/pad of the broadcast view yields the same data, just one fewer copy). Expected mechanism: one fewer large copy kernel + bytes moved per predict in the copy/launch-bound multiclass decoder.

In ICLAttention's non-cached path, x_train = x_BRE[:, :N] is a row-strided (non-contiguous) view when N<R. It feeds two nn.Linear calls (k_projection, v_projection); each F.linear flattens to 2D, forcing an implicit aten::copy_ of the strided slice — i.e. it's materialized twice per ICL layer. Calling .contiguous() once makes both subsequent projections' reshapes free, eliminating one large copy per ICL layer in the copy/launch-bound regime. Output is bit-identical (contiguous changes layout only; no-op when N==R). Expected mechanism: fewer aten::copy_ kernels + bytes moved across all ICL layers (this is the hot path for both typical and sample operating points, which route heavily through ICL attention).

Remove the redundant per-block `.contiguous()` on the InducedSelfAttentionBlock output (B,R,C,E). The returned tensor is always re-materialized by its consumer — the next dist-embedder block immediately does `x_BRCE.transpose(1,2).contiguous()`, and the final block's output is consumed by ColumnAggregator's `torch.cat((cls, x), dim=2)`, which copies into a fresh contiguous buffer regardless. So the trailing `.contiguous()` is a redundant large copy (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op at 7.97ms/1886 calls). Dropping it removes one full-size copy kernel per dist-embedder block per row-chunk in the launch/copy-bound eager (typical) path, while the compiled (sample) path is neutral (inductor already fuses contiguity into the downstream op, and large-row sample is GPU-bound). Output-preserving: only contiguity differs; all values identical (same proven pattern as the accepted SDPA-`.contiguous()` removal).

In Attention.forward (the self-attention used by ColumnAggregator's TransformerBlocks), q/k/v are computed as three separate nn.Linear GEMMs on the SAME input x_BSE with identical output dims. Concatenating the three (bias-free) weight matrices into one and issuing a single F.linear + split is mathematically/bit-identical to three independent matmuls (it's just a block-structured GEMM), but issues 1 GEMM kernel instead of 3 per call, reducing launches in the GEMM-heavy region (aten::mm 3.98ms/800 + aten::addmm 1.86ms). The concatenated weight is frozen at inference, so it is cached on the module (keyed on the source weight identity + ._version) to avoid rebuilding it each call; under torch.compiler.is_compiling() it is rebuilt inline so the compiled (sample) graph stays a pure function with no python-side-effect graph break. The q/k/v split slices the contiguous last dim, then unflatten/reshape into (B,S,H,D) keeps last-dim stride 1 (flash-eligible) and the downstream RoPE/SDPA math is unchanged. Expected: fewer GEMM launches → faster typical (eager) and neutral-or-faster sample.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward recomputes `base_scales = base_mlp(_safe_log_seqlen(n))` on every scaled-attention call, but base_scales depends ONLY on (n, dtype, device) and the frozen inference weights — never on the query. That tiny chain (log + two Linears + GELU + view ≈ 8 launch-overhead-bound kernels) runs per ICL block, per dist-embedder cross-attn block, and the decoder, with the SAME n across the harness's repeated timed predicts. Caching it bit-exactly eliminates those launches in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. This is materially different from the previously-rejected always-on base_scales cache: that version regressed `sample` (0.9971, confidently slower) because the large-input path's ICL blocks run EAGER with large n and paid the per-call dict-lookup cost. Here the cache is GATED to small n (< 8192, mirroring the compile size-gate) using a single-slot tuple (cheaper than a dict), so the large/sample path takes the no-cache branch (just one int compare) and stays neutral, while typical (small n) gets the full ~6% win. Bit-exact: the cached tensor is exactly what base_mlp recomputes (deterministic, frozen weights); the per-query path (query_mlp/tanh/mul) is unchanged. Cache is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() and training, so no compiled graph is perturbed. Expected: typical clearly >3% faster, sample neutral.

RMSNorm is the launch storm: torch<2.12's eager F.rms_norm decomposes into ~5 separate kernels (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul). The profiler's aten::mean x400, aten::pow x403, aten::rsqrt x398 counts match ~398 RMSNorm calls/predict — i.e. ~1600+ tiny kernel launches purely from normalization in the launch-bound (GPU 25-60% busy) regime. Fuse them by compiling F.rms_norm ONCE with dynamic=True (so the varying batch/row dims don't recompile) into a single Inductor triton kernel, reused across every _DtypeMatchingRMSNorm instance. This collapses ~5 launches → 1 per norm call. Gated to `not self.training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`: inside the existing sample-path compiled subgraphs the outer graph already fuses (is_compiling True → plain F.rms_norm, unchanged), while the eager ICL-loop/output norms (run eager even on the sample path) and the entire typical path get the fused kernel. Output-preserving: same rms-norm math, only op-fusion/fp-reordering that the gate already tolerates for the compiled sample path. Distinct compile variants are bounded (2 normalized_shapes × weight-presence), well under cache_size_limit.

Stage-3 ICL transformer blocks run eager even on the sample (>=8192 rows) path where torch.compile is already enabled and proven (+8.6% from compiling stages 0-2). Each ICLTransformerBlock issues the RMSNorm launch-storm (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul x N), Linear projections, softmax-scaling pointwise, and MLP as dozens of tiny eager kernels per layer per predict — pure launch overhead in this 25-60%-GPU-busy regime. Extend the existing compile coverage to the ICL loop: compile each block once (keyed on module id, since all blocks share the same forward code object but have distinct weights — reusing _compiled's __func__ key would wrongly alias them) with dynamic=True and mark_dynamic on the batch/row dims so varying test-row counts don't recompile. num_train stays a python-int guard (fixed per operating point) so the train-key slice point is static. This collapses the per-block launch storm into fused Inductor kernels, the same mechanism that already sped up stages 0-2. Strictly scoped to enable_torch_compile (set False for <8192 rows at line 1749), so the eager 'typical' path is byte-identical and only the already-compile-tolerant 'sample' path is touched (fp-reordering within the gate's tolerance). force_recompute and KV-cache/return_kv paths are left untouched. _compiled_module cache is popped in __getstate__ for picklability like the existing cache.

Fused QKV/KV projections to cut GEMM launches in the launch-bound eager attention path. Each attention module (Attention, CrossAttention, ICLAttention) currently issues separate nn.Linear matmuls for projections that share the SAME input tensor: Attention projects q,k,v all from x_BSE (3 matmuls); CrossAttention projects k,v both from the key/value input (2 matmuls); ICLAttention projects k,v both from x_train (2 matmuls). Concatenating those weights along the output dim and doing ONE F.linear, then slicing the result, is mathematically identical (each output row is the same dot product) and removes 1-2 aten::mm/addmm launches per attention call. These modules run eager — the dist-embedder's CrossAttention (InducedSelfAttentionBlock x2/layer x column-chunks) and column-aggregator's self-Attention dominate the eager 'typical' path, and the ICL blocks run eager on both paths — so this directly attacks the launch-overhead (GPU 25-60% busy). The concatenated weight is cached on the module (keyed on each weight's _version/dtype/device, like the accepted base_scales cache), so torch.cat runs once and is reused across all timed predicts. The fused path is disabled under torch.compiler.is_compiling() and training, so the compiled stages-0-2 'sample' subgraphs stay byte-identical (no graph break, no recompile) and only eager regions get fewer launches. ICLAttention skips fusion when return_kv (cache-build) so the KV-cache entries keep owning their own contiguous storage (no memory-retention behaviour change). Output-preserving: only the GEMM tiling/fp-reordering may differ, which the argmax+JS gate tolerates.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886 calls) in this launch-bound regime. The dist-embedder stacks N InducedSelfAttentionBlock layers; each block's forward does `transpose(1,2).contiguous()` on entry (BRCE->BCRE) and again on exit (BCRE->BRCE). Between consecutive layers these two large layout copies are exact inverses that cancel — pure wasted bandwidth+launches. Chain the blocks directly in the flattened (B*C, R, E) layout so the round-trip collapses to ONE transpose+contiguous at entry and ONE at exit, eliminating 2*(num_layers-1) big copies per dist-embedder pass. Bit-identical: transpose+contiguous is a pure layout copy that never changes values, and the per-block induced-attention math operates on the same logical (B*C,R,E) values regardless of how the buffer was produced. Strictly gated to the eager inference fast path (not training, not compiling, no KV cache / hidden / recompute), so the compiled `sample` subgraph (_process_row_chunk) is byte-identical — only the eager `typical` path gets fewer copies. forward_flat reuses the existing chunked_evaluate_maybe_inplace + _induced_attention exactly. Expected: fewer large copy_ kernels + bytes moved -> faster typical, neutral sample.

Dist-embedder cross_attn_block1's query is always the frozen inducing_vectors (unsqueeze+expand → shares storage), so layernorm_q(ind)+q_projection(ind) is data-independent and bit-identical every call. In the eager 'typical' path this block runs per dist-embedder-block × per column-chunk × per predict, each time relaunching the RMSNorm storm + a GEMM purely to recompute a constant query. Add a `query_is_constant` flag to CrossAttentionBlock that caches the projected query q (B,Q,H,D) keyed on (inducing storage ptr, shape, dtype) and passes it to CrossAttention via a new optional `precomputed_q`, skipping layernorm_q + q_projection on hits. Cache is gated to `not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, so the compiled sample graph traces the unchanged path (precomputed_q stays None) and is byte-identical. Output-preserving: precomputed_q equals the recomputed normed+projected query exactly (frozen weights, identical input values; expand makes all batch rows identical). Mechanism: collapses many redundant RMSNorm/GEMM launches per predict in the launch-bound regime; neutral on sample.
